papers.title: On the spatial distribution and the origin of hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: Youjun Lu Fupeng Zhang Qingjuan Yu

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) escaping away from the Galactic halo are dynamical products of interactions of stars with the massive black hole(s) (MBH) in the Galactic Center (GC). They are mainly B-type stars with their progenitors unknown. OB stars are also populated in the GC, with many being hosted in a clockwise-rotating young stellar (CWS) disk within half a parsec from the MBH and their formation remaining puzzles. In this paper, we demonstrate that HVSs can well memorize the injecting directions of their progenitors using both analytical arguments and numerical simulations, i.e., the ejecting direction of an HVS is almost anti-parallel to the injecting direction of its progenitor. Therefore, the spatial distribution of HVSs maps the spatial distribution of the parent population of their progenitors directly. We also find that almost all the discovered HVSs are spatially consistent with being located on two thin disk planes. The orientation of one plane is consistent with that of the (inner) CWS disk, which suggests that most of the HVSs originate from the CWS disk or a previously existed disk-like stellar structure with an orientation similar to it. The rest of HVSs may be correlated with the plane of the northern arm of the mini-spiral in the GC or the plane defined by the outer warped part of the CWS disk. Our results not only support the GC origin of HVSs but also imply that the central disk (or the disk structure with a similar orientation) should persist or be frequently rejuvenated over the past 200 Myr, which adds a new challenge to the stellar disk formation and provides insights to the longstanding problem of gas fueling into massive black holes.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper demonstrates that hypervelocity stars (HVSs) retain a memory of their progenitor's injection direction, with their ejecting velocity nearly anti-parallel to the progenitor's direction, enabling their spatial distribution to directly map the parent population's geometry. Observational analysis shows that nearly all known HVSs align with two thin disk planes: one matching the orientation of the clockwise-rotating young stellar disk (CWS) near the Galactic Center, and another aligned with the northern arm or outer warped part of the CWS disk, implying persistent or recurrent disk-like structures in the GC over the past 200 Myr, which challenges current models of stellar disk formation and gas accretion onto the central black hole.

source_fields.title: On the spatial distribution and the origin of hypervelocity stars

source_fields.sections_text: 4. Discussion: The discovered HVSs exhibit a spatial distribution aligned with two disk planes—consistent with the inner CWS disk and the Narm or outer warped part of the CWS disk—suggesting a possible origin from these structures, though the persistence of young stellar formation in these regions over $\\sim 10^8$ years remains puzzling and highlights the need for continuous gas accretion and dynamical perturbations to explain their formation and orbital evolution. 5. Conclusions: HVSs retain a memory of their progenitor's injecting direction, indicating that their spatial distribution reflects the structure of the parent disk population, with most originating from the inner CWS disk or related disk-like structures, suggesting a persistent or recurrent disk in the Galactic Center over the past 200 Myr. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that accounts for the anisotropic distribution of hypervelocity stars by considering their progenitors from young, structured populations in the Galactic Center—such as the clockwise stellar disk and young clusters—rather than assuming isotropic injection, thereby solving the problem of reconciling observed HVS spatial distributions with dynamical ejection mechanisms. 3. Comparison with observations: The spatial distribution of hypervelocity stars shows a strong preference for alignment with the clockwise young stellar disk plane, with most lying within a few degrees of it, supporting their origin from ejection processes related to this structure. 2. The deflection angle: the direction change of HVSs from its progenitor: HVSs ejected via tidal breakup of binary stars retain a memory of their progenitor's injecting direction, with the deflection angle being small and predictable based on the binary's initial orbit and the gravitational interaction with the central black hole.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) escaping away from the Galactic halo are dynamical products of interactions of stars with the massive black hole(s) (MBH) in the Galactic Center (GC).
They are mainly B-type stars with their progenitors unknown.
OB stars are also populated in the GC, with many being hosted in a clockwise-rotating young stellar (CWS) disk within half a parsec from the MBH and their formation remaining puzzles.
title: On the spatial distribution and the origin of hypervelocity stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes spatial distribution of 'almost all the discovered HVSs' and states 'observational analysis shows that nearly all known HVSs align with two thin disk planes', indicating use of existing observational catalog of hypervelocity stars.
